# OUR RESEARCH
Selected research papers published by 1QBit’s team and collaborators
## [Neural Error Mitigation of Near-Term Quantum Simulations](https://1qbit.com/research/neural-error-mitigation-of-near-term-quantum-simulations/)
By Elizabeth R. Bennewitz, Florian Hopfmueller, Bohdan Kulchytskyy, Juan Carrasquilla, & Pooya Ronagh
One of the promising applications of early quantum computers is the simulation of quantum systems. Variational methods for near-term quantum computers, such as the variational quantum eigensolver (VQE), are a promising approach to finding ground states of quantum systems relevant in physics, chemistry, and materials science…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/neural-error-mitigation-of-near-term-quantum-simulations/)
## [Benchmark Study of Quantum Algorithms for Combinatorial Optimization: Unitary versus Dissipative](https://1qbit.com/research/benchmark-study-of-quantum-algorithms-for-combinatorial-optimization-unitary-versus-dissipative/)
By Krishanu Sankar, Artur Scherer, Satoshi Kako, Sam Reifenstein, Navid Ghadermarzy, Willem B. Krayenhoff, Yoshitaka Inui, Edwin Ng, Tatsuhiro Onodera, Pooya Ronagh, & Yoshihisa Yamamoto
We study the performance scaling of three quantum algorithms for combinatorial optimization: measurement-feedback coherent Ising machines (MFB-CIM), discrete adiabatic quantum computation (DAQC), and the Dürr-Hoyer algorithm for quantum minimum finding (DH-QMF) that is based on Grover’s search. We use MaxCut problems as our reference for comparison, and time-to-solution (TTS) as a practical measure of performance for these optimization algorithms…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/benchmark-study-of-quantum-algorithms-for-combinatorial-optimization-unitary-versus-dissipative/)
## [Scaling Up Electronic Structure Calculations on Quantum Computers: The Frozen Natural Orbital Based Method of Increments](https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-electronic-structure-calculations-quantum-computers-frozen-natural-orbital-based-method-increments/)
By Prakash Verma, Lee Huntington, Marc Coons, Yukio Kawashima, Takeshi Yamazaki, & Arman Zaribafiyan
The method of increments and frozen natural orbital (MI-FNO) framework is introduced to help expedite the application of noisy, intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices for quantum chemistry simulations. The MI-FNO framework provides a systematic reduction of the occupied and virtual orbital spaces for quantum chemistry simulations. The correlation energies of the resulting increments from the MI-FNO reduction can then be solved by various algorithms, including quantum algorithms such as the phase estimation algorithm and the variational quantum eigensolver (VQE)…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-electronic-structure-calculations-quantum-computers-frozen-natural-orbital-based-method-increments/)
## [Variationally Scheduled Quantum Simulation](https://1qbit.com/research/variationally-scheduled-quantum-simulation/)
By Shunji Matsuura, Samantha Buck, Valentin Senicourt, & Arman Zaribafiyan
Eigenstate preparation is ubiquitous in quantum computing, and a standard approach for generating the lowest-energy states of a given system is by employing adiabatic state preparation (ASP). In the present work, we investigate a variational method for determining the optimal scheduling procedure within the context of ASP. In the absence of quantum error correction, running a quantum device for any meaningful amount of time causes a system to become susceptible to the loss of relevant information…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/variationally-scheduled-quantum-simulation/)
## [Efficient and Accurate Electronic Structure Simulation Demonstrated on a Trapped-Ion Quantum Computer](https://1qbit.com/research/efficient-and-accurate-electronic-structure-simulation-demonstrated-on-a-trapped-ion-quantum-computer/)
By Yukio Kawashima, Marc P. Coons, Yunseong Nam, Erika Lloyd, Shunji Matsuura, Alejandro J. Garza, Sonika Johri, Lee Huntington, Valentin Senicourt, Andrii O. Maksymov, Jason H. V. Nguyen, Jungsang Kim, Nima Alidoust, Arman Zaribafiyan, & Takeshi Yamazaki
Quantum computers have the potential to perform accurate and efficient electronic structure calculations, enabling the simulation of properties of materials. However, today’s noisy, intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices have a limited number of qubits and gate operations due to the presence of errors. Here, we propose a systematically improvable end-to-end pipeline to alleviate these limitations…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/efficient-and-accurate-electronic-structure-simulation-demonstrated-on-a-trapped-ion-quantum-computer/)
## [Scaling Overhead of Locality Reduction in Binary Optimization Problems](https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-overhead-of-locality-reduction-in-binary-optimization-problems/)
By Elisabetta Valiante, Maritza Hernandez, Amin Barzegar, & Helmut G. Katzgraber
Recently, there has been considerable interest in solving optimization problems by mapping these onto a binary representation, sparked mostly by the use of quantum annealing machines. Such binary representation is reminiscent of a discrete physical two-state system, such as the Ising model. As such, physics-inspired techniques—commonly used in fundamental physics studies—are ideally suited to solve optimization problems in a binary format…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-overhead-of-locality-reduction-in-binary-optimization-problems/)
## [Quantum Multiple Kernel Learning](https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-multiple-kernel-learning/)
By Seyed Shakib Vedaie, Moslem Noori, Jaspreet S. Oberoi, Barry C. Sanders, & Ehsan Zahedinejad
Kernel methods play an important role in machine learning applications due to their conceptual simplicity and superior performance on numerous machine learning tasks. Expressivity of a machine learning model, referring to the ability of the model to approximate complex functions, has a significant influence on its performance in these tasks. One approach to enhancing the expressivity of kernel machines is to combine multiple individual kernels to arrive at a more expressive combined kernel…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-multiple-kernel-learning/)
## [Quantum Annealing Approaches to the Phase-Unwrapping Problem in Synthetic-Aperture Radar Imaging](https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-annealing-approaches-to-the-phase-unwrapping-problem-in-synthetic-aperture-radar-imaging/)
By Khaled A. Helal Kelany, Nikitas Dimopoulos, Clemens P. J. Adolphs, Bardia Barabadi, & Amirali Baniasadi
The focus of this work is to explore the use of quantum annealing solvers for the problem of phase unwrapping of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images. Although solutions to this problem exist based on network programming, these techniques do not scale well to larger-sized images. Our approach involves formulating the problem as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem, which can be solved using a quantum annealer…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-annealing-approaches-to-the-phase-unwrapping-problem-in-synthetic-aperture-radar-imaging/)
## [Finding the Ground State of Spin Hamiltonians with Reinforcement Learning](https://1qbit.com/research/finding-the-ground-state-of-spin-hamiltonians-with-reinforcement-learning/)
By Kyle Mills, Pooya Ronagh, & Isaac Tamblyn
Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a proven method for optimizing a procedure for which success has been defined, but the specific actions needed to achieve it have not. Using a method we call “controlled online optimization learning” (COOL), we apply the so-called “black box” method of RL to simulated annealing (SA), demonstrating that an RL agent based on proximal policy optimization can, through experience alone, arrive at a temperature schedule that surpasses the performance of standard heuristic temperature schedules for two classes of Hamiltonians…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/finding-the-ground-state-of-spin-hamiltonians-with-reinforcement-learning/)
## [Quantum Algorithms for Solving Dynamic Programming Problems](https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-algorithms-for-solving-dynamic-programming-problems/)
By Pooya Ronagh
We present a general quantum algorithm for solving finite-horizon dynamic programming problems. Up to polylogarithmic factors, our algorithm provides a quadratic quantum advantage in terms of the number of states of a given dynamic programming problem. This speedup comes at the expense of the appearance of other polynomial factors representative of the number of actions of the dynamic programming problem, the maximum value of the instantaneous reward, and the time horizon of the problem…
[read more](https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-algorithms-for-solving-dynamic-programming-problems/)

			<article id="post-10066" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_0 post-10066 research type-research status-publish hentry category-quantum-computing">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/neural-error-mitigation-of-near-term-quantum-simulations/">Neural Error Mitigation of Near-Term Quantum Simulations</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Elizabeth R. Bennewitz, Florian Hopfmueller, Bohdan Kulchytskyy, Juan Carrasquilla, & Pooya Ronagh</p>
<p>One of the promising applications of early quantum computers is the simulation of quantum systems. Variational methods for near-term quantum computers, such as the variational quantum eigensolver (VQE), are a promising approach to finding ground states of quantum systems relevant in physics, chemistry, and materials science…</p>
</div><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/neural-error-mitigation-of-near-term-quantum-simulations/" class="more-link">read more</a></div>

			<article id="post-9987" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_1 post-9987 research type-research status-publish hentry category-quantum-computing">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/benchmark-study-of-quantum-algorithms-for-combinatorial-optimization-unitary-versus-dissipative/">Benchmark Study of Quantum Algorithms for Combinatorial Optimization: Unitary versus Dissipative</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Krishanu Sankar, Artur Scherer, Satoshi Kako, Sam Reifenstein, Navid Ghadermarzy, Willem B. Krayenhoff, Yoshitaka Inui, Edwin Ng, Tatsuhiro Onodera, Pooya Ronagh, & Yoshihisa Yamamoto</p>
<p>We study the performance scaling of three quantum algorithms for combinatorial optimization: measurement-feedback coherent Ising machines (MFB-CIM), discrete adiabatic quantum computation (DAQC), and the Dürr-Hoyer algorithm for quantum minimum finding (DH-QMF) that is based on Grover’s search. We use MaxCut problems as our reference for comparison, and time-to-solution (TTS) as a practical measure of performance for these optimization algorithms…</p>
</div><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/benchmark-study-of-quantum-algorithms-for-combinatorial-optimization-unitary-versus-dissipative/" class="more-link">read more</a></div>			
			</article> <!-- .et_pb_post -->
				
			<article id="post-4453" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_2 post-4453 research type-research status-publish hentry">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-electronic-structure-calculations-quantum-computers-frozen-natural-orbital-based-method-increments/">Scaling Up Electronic Structure Calculations on Quantum Computers: The Frozen Natural Orbital Based Method of Increments</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Prakash Verma, Lee Huntington, Marc Coons, Yukio Kawashima, Takeshi Yamazaki, & Arman Zaribafiyan</p>
<p>The method of increments and frozen natural orbital (MI-FNO) framework is introduced to help expedite the application of noisy, intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices for quantum chemistry simulations. The MI-FNO framework provides a systematic reduction of the occupied and virtual orbital spaces for quantum chemistry simulations. The correlation energies of the resulting increments from the MI-FNO reduction can then be solved by various algorithms, including quantum algorithms such as the phase estimation algorithm and the variational quantum eigensolver (VQE)…</p>
</div><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-electronic-structure-calculations-quantum-computers-frozen-natural-orbital-based-method-increments/" class="more-link">read more</a></div>			
			</article> <!-- .et_pb_post -->
				
			<article id="post-4456" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_3 post-4456 research type-research status-publish hentry">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/variationally-scheduled-quantum-simulation/">Variationally Scheduled Quantum Simulation</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Shunji Matsuura, Samantha Buck, Valentin Senicourt, & Arman Zaribafiyan</p>
<p>Eigenstate preparation is ubiquitous in quantum computing, and a standard approach for generating the lowest-energy states of a given system is by employing adiabatic state preparation (ASP). In the present work, we investigate a variational method for determining the optimal scheduling procedure within the context of ASP. In the absence of quantum error correction, running a quantum device for any meaningful amount of time causes a system to become susceptible to the loss of relevant information…</p>

			</article> <!-- .et_pb_post -->

														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/efficient-and-accurate-electronic-structure-simulation-demonstrated-on-a-trapped-ion-quantum-computer/">Efficient and Accurate Electronic Structure Simulation Demonstrated on a Trapped-Ion Quantum Computer</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Yukio Kawashima, Marc P. Coons, Yunseong Nam, Erika Lloyd, Shunji Matsuura, Alejandro J. Garza, Sonika Johri, Lee Huntington, Valentin Senicourt, Andrii O. Maksymov, Jason H. V. Nguyen, Jungsang Kim, Nima Alidoust, Arman Zaribafiyan, & Takeshi Yamazaki</p>
<p>Quantum computers have the potential to perform accurate and efficient electronic structure calculations, enabling the simulation of properties of materials. However, today’s noisy, intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices have a limited number of qubits and gate operations due to the presence of errors. Here, we propose a systematically improvable end-to-end pipeline to alleviate these limitations…</p>
</div><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/efficient-and-accurate-electronic-structure-simulation-demonstrated-on-a-trapped-ion-quantum-computer/" class="more-link">read more</a></div>

			<article id="post-7314" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_5 post-7314 research type-research status-publish hentry">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-overhead-of-locality-reduction-in-binary-optimization-problems/">Scaling Overhead of Locality Reduction in Binary Optimization Problems</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Elisabetta Valiante, Maritza Hernandez, Amin Barzegar, & Helmut G. Katzgraber</p>
<p>Recently, there has been considerable interest in solving optimization problems by mapping these onto a binary representation, sparked mostly by the use of quantum annealing machines. Such binary representation is reminiscent of a discrete physical two-state system, such as the Ising model. As such, physics-inspired techniques—commonly used in fundamental physics studies—are ideally suited to solve optimization problems in a binary format…</p>
</div><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/scaling-overhead-of-locality-reduction-in-binary-optimization-problems/" class="more-link">read more</a></div>

			<article id="post-7313" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_6 post-7313 research type-research status-publish hentry">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-multiple-kernel-learning/">Quantum Multiple Kernel Learning</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Seyed Shakib Vedaie, Moslem Noori, Jaspreet S. Oberoi, Barry C. Sanders, & Ehsan Zahedinejad</p>
<p>Kernel methods play an important role in machine learning applications due to their conceptual simplicity and superior performance on numerous machine learning tasks. Expressivity of a machine learning model, referring to the ability of the model to approximate complex functions, has a significant influence on its performance in these tasks. One approach to enhancing the expressivity of kernel machines is to combine multiple individual kernels to arrive at a more expressive combined kernel…</p>

			<article id="post-7277" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_7 post-7277 research type-research status-publish hentry">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-annealing-approaches-to-the-phase-unwrapping-problem-in-synthetic-aperture-radar-imaging/">Quantum Annealing Approaches to the Phase-Unwrapping Problem in Synthetic-Aperture Radar Imaging</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Khaled A. Helal Kelany, Nikitas Dimopoulos, Clemens P. J. Adolphs, Bardia Barabadi, & Amirali Baniasadi</p>
<p>The focus of this work is to explore the use of quantum annealing solvers for the problem of phase unwrapping of synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images. Although solutions to this problem exist based on network programming, these techniques do not scale well to larger-sized images. Our approach involves formulating the problem as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem, which can be solved using a quantum annealer…</p>
</div><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-annealing-approaches-to-the-phase-unwrapping-problem-in-synthetic-aperture-radar-imaging/" class="more-link">read more</a></div>

			<article id="post-7276" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_8 post-7276 research type-research status-publish hentry">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/finding-the-ground-state-of-spin-hamiltonians-with-reinforcement-learning/">Finding the Ground State of Spin Hamiltonians with Reinforcement Learning</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Kyle Mills, Pooya Ronagh, & Isaac Tamblyn</p>
<p>Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a proven method for optimizing a procedure for which success has been defined, but the specific actions needed to achieve it have not. Using a method we call “controlled online optimization learning” (COOL), we apply the so-called “black box” method of RL to simulated annealing (SA), demonstrating that an RL agent based on proximal policy optimization can, through experience alone, arrive at a temperature schedule that surpasses the performance of standard heuristic temperature schedules for two classes of Hamiltonians…</p>

			</article> <!-- .et_pb_post -->
				
			<article id="post-7134" class="et_pb_post clearfix et_pb_no_thumb et_pb_blog_item_0_9 post-7134 research type-research status-publish hentry">

				
														<h2 class="entry-title"><a href="https://1qbit.com/research/quantum-algorithms-for-solving-dynamic-programming-problems/">Quantum Algorithms for Solving Dynamic Programming Problems</a></h2>
				
					<p class="post-meta"></p><div class="post-content"><div class="post-content-inner"><p>By Pooya Ronagh</p>
<p>We present a general quantum algorithm for solving finite-horizon dynamic programming problems. Up to polylogarithmic factors, our algorithm provides a quadratic quantum advantage in terms of the number of states of a given dynamic programming problem. This speedup comes at the expense of the appearance of other polynomial factors representative of the number of actions of the dynamic programming problem, the maximum value of the instantaneous reward, and the time horizon of the problem…</p>
